name: Nazca Lines Full
description: The Nazca Lines, located in the Nazca Desert of southern Peru, are a collection of hundreds of geoglyphs – large designs etched into the ground. The glyphs range from simple lines to complex depictions of animals, plants, and geometric shapes. Mainstream archaeology attributes their creation to the Nazca culture, who flourished in the area between 1 and 700 AD. The prevailing theory suggests they were created for ritualistic purposes, possibly related to agriculture, water cycles, and astronomical observations. Proponents of alternative theories propose explanations beyond simple ritual, often suggesting advanced technology, extraterrestrial involvement, or lost civilizations. These theories cite the scale and precision of the lines, the depiction of seemingly non-native animals, and the perceived lack of a clear practical purpose as evidence. However, mainstream scholars argue that the Nazca people possessed the necessary skills and knowledge to create the lines using simple tools and techniques, such as surveying and rope-and-stake methods. The alternative theories often lack concrete evidence and rely on speculation and misinterpretations of archaeological data. While the exact meaning and purpose of the Nazca Lines remain a subject of debate, mainstream research continues to uncover valuable insights into the Nazca culture and their worldview, without resorting to extraordinary explanations. The lines' preservation is an ongoing concern, threatened by erosion, tourism, and development.
subtitle: Ancient geoglyphs of the Nazca Desert

claims.claim: The Nazca Lines were created with advanced technology.
claims.evidence: The size and precision of the lines suggest advanced surveying techniques beyond the capabilities of the Nazca people.
claims.criticism: Archaeological evidence demonstrates the Nazca people's ability to create the lines using simple tools and techniques, such as rope, stakes, and surveying knowledge. The perceived precision is often exaggerated.

claims.claim: The Nazca Lines served as landing strips for extraterrestrial spacecraft.
claims.evidence: Some of the lines are long and straight, resembling runways. Erich von Däniken popularized this theory.
claims.criticism: This theory lacks any scientific evidence. The lines are not suitable for landing aircraft due to their uneven surface and surrounding terrain. Their length is also not adequate for modern aircraft.

claims.claim: The Nazca Lines are connected to ancient astronomical knowledge and were used as an astronomical calendar.
claims.evidence: Some lines appear to align with solstices and equinoxes.
claims.criticism: While some lines may have astronomical alignments, the majority do not. The claim that the entire complex served as a comprehensive calendar is not supported by evidence. The alignments could be coincidental or have other ritualistic significance.

keyFigures.name: Maria Reiche
keyFigures.role: Dedicated her life to studying and preserving the Nazca Lines.

keyFigures.name: Erich von Däniken
keyFigures.role: Popularized the extraterrestrial theory in his book 'Chariots of the Gods?'

keyFigures.name: Anthony Aveni
keyFigures.role: Archaeoastronomer who studied the astronomical significance of the Nazca Lines.

timeline.event: Pedro Cieza de León first mentions seeing the lines in his chronicle.
The lines are rediscovered by modern aviation.
Maria Reiche begins her extensive research and preservation efforts.
Erich von Däniken publishes 'Chariots of the Gods?', popularizing the extraterrestrial theory.
The Nazca Lines are designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

sources.author: Aveni, Anthony F.
sources.work: Between the Lines: The Mystery of the Giant Ground Drawings of Ancient Nazca, Peru
sources.title: Aveni, Anthony F.

sources.author: Silverman, Helaine
sources.work: Cahuachi in the Ancient Nasca World
sources.title: Silverman, Helaine
